figures.caption: Figure 2| Specialized GO Enrichment Analysis Workflow for Disease Enrichment Analysis. Schematic illustration of the bidirectional comparative framework used to identify biological processes differentially enriched between IBD and healthy metagenomes following microbial–human gene mapping. UniProtderived human Ensembl gene sets were independently generated for IBD and healthy cohorts and subjected to GO enrichment analysis using g:Profiler. Enrichment differences were quantified by calculating Δ(-log₁₀ p-value) between cohorts in a bidirectional manner (IBD-Healthy and Healthy-IBD). For IBD-Healthy comparisons, three analytical layers were applied: (i) basic GO enrichment comparison, (ii) a 3-filter strategy restricting analysis to Biological Process terms, specialized (non-generalized) pathways, and GO terms annotated to fewer than 500 genes, and (iii) a 2-filter strategy in which the pathway specialization filter was relaxed to capture development-related processes. For Healthy–IBD comparisons, a modified 2- filter strategy was implemented due to the absence of significant hits under stringent gene-count thresholds, allowing inclusion of broadly annotated GO terms (≤10,000 genes) to capture enrichment of core biological processes in healthy individuals. This multi-tiered filtering and Δ-based comparative approach enables robust identification of disease-specific versus health-associated functional signatures arising from microbial molecular mimicry.

Figure 6 | Comparative distribution of PFAM protein families in Healthy and IBD gut metagenomes.

Figure 1 | Overview of the Molecular Mimicry In-silico Pipeline (MMIP). Schematic representation of the MMIP workflow used to identify bacterial proteins exhibiting functional mimicry of human pathways from gut metagenomic datasets. Raw shotgun metagenomic reads undergo stringent quality control, followed by de novo assembly and open reading frame (ORF) prediction. Predicted protein sequences are queried against the NCBI non-redundant database using BLASTP, and top-hit homologs are subjected to UniProt ID mapping to retrieve functional metadata, including Gene Ontology (GO) annotations. UniProtderived GO terms are subsequently mapped to corresponding human Ensembl gene IDs. Functional characterization is performed through two complementary approaches: GO enrichment analysis using g:Profiler to identify statistically overrepresented biological processes, and frequency-based functional representation analysis to quantify and compare the abundance of human-mimicking genes and GO terms between IBD and healthy cohorts. The pipeline further integrates PFAM domain annotation to identify conserved protein families and validates bacterial origin of selected mimicry signals through taxonomically restricted BLASTP and abundance profiling. Collectively, MMIP enables systematic detection and interpretation of cross-kingdom molecular mimicry linking gut microbial proteins to host functional pathways.
